Net written premiums were $11.317B, up 8% year over year, as the company saw growth in all three reported segments. Net income was $1.26B, with core income constituting the bulk, $1.218B. The underwriting profit margin came in at around 13.25%, with underlying underwriting income up 73% y/y, at $1.5B.

On a per-share basis, the company saw a whopping 211% increase in net income per diluted share to $5.42, while core income per diluted share was up 169% at $5.24. Book value per share was up 39% y/y to $122, while adjusted book value per share was up 13% to $131.30.

One of the main metrics for insurance companies, the return on equity, came in at 19.2%, with core ROE at 16.6%. ROE last year was 7.7%. The company turned to underwriting profits compared to last year, with a combined ratio coming in at 93.2%, or 7.8 points lower than last year. If we strip out the factors that distort the company's underwriting profitability, like the catastrophe losses, favorable development on prior-year losses, and certain investment gains/losses, the company's underlying combined ratio came in at an excellent 85.6%. This was a whopping 5% improvement on last year.

These results seem like night and day compared to the same period last year. It could be said that because the results last year were terrible, an easy comp is not surprising, but the company has delivered above and beyond with growth and efficiency improvements across the board.

Comments on the Performance

The company's main financial metrics are heading in the right direction. With book value per share coming in at $122, the company's P/B sits at around 2.15. Some people may think this is a little high, but if we look at the competition, as provided by default by Seeking Alpha, TRV's P/B ratio is somewhere close to the lowest. This means the company is not as expensive as the other players in the sector. The company is more than capable of increasing its book value considerably y/y, so this is rewarded by investors, and a P/B ratio of 2.2 reflects the company's ability to compound book value per share.

In terms of return on equity, the company is close to what I would consider quite profitable, especially after this recent report. The company's ROE came in just under what I would like to see, which is 20%. However, when we look at the same period last year, we can see a tremendous improvement. I would like to see the company maintain this sort of profitability over the long run. Furthermore, compared to the peers, and I believe SA's figures are not yet updated with Q3 results, TRV should rank a little higher than where it is shown in the image below, considering the recent improvements y/y.

I am also very impressed with how much the company's combined ratio has improved from just a year ago. If you are new to insurance companies (which I am too), the lower the combined ratio, the more profitable the insurance company is. What that measures is the underwriting expenses and loss payments relative to total earned premiums, so if expenses total less than what the company managed to earn in premiums, that means the company is profitable. Last year, the company was above 100%, which meant it was making an underwriting loss. An ideal underwriting profit margin is somewhere between 10% and 15%, and this quarter, the company was bang in the middle of it, which tells me that the company is very efficient at making a profit.

Another important metric to look at is how the company uses the float available, which is the difference between the premiums received and claims paid out. This is cash that is not the insurance company's but is invested to generate income. This was 16% higher than last year, driven by what looks like a reliable return on the company's growing fixed-income portfolio, which makes up most of the portfolio, and equity securities performed well during the same period. Of course, an insurance company cannot play around with other people's money and invest in risky assets. That's why equity securities sit at $652m in fair value, while fixed income is at around $84B, with quite a bit of unrealized losses during the period. Nevertheless, the company consistently bags around 3% from these investments, which is decent and risk-free.

Now that the Fed has begun cutting interest rates, it will be interesting to see how this will affect the company's fixed-income portfolio. Usually, as interest rates fall, the value of existing government bonds increases. Those unrealized losses that we see in the company's 10-Q should start to reverse, but I expect lower quarterly income going forward since because of these lower interest rates.

Comments on the Outlook

About half of the company's catastrophe losses came in from Hurricane Helene, which impacted the company financially in Georgia and the Carolinas regions the most, while the company did not experience much financial burden in Florida. Hurricane Helene made landfall just north-west of Florida in the Big Bend coastal region, which has relatively low population density, so it makes sense that the company did not suffer much financial burden in the region; however, Hurricane Milton made landfall in Florida's central west coast as a category three hurricane. On the Q3 earnings call, the management said it is still early to report the costs of Milton, but so far, the damages stand between $75m and $175m pre-tax. Looking at the trajectories of the two hurricanes, I think Helene will cost a lot more than Milton since it hit a lot more states, whereas Milton mostly hit Florida. I know some people who traveled to South Carolina during the hurricane, and for the most part, South Carolina was fine. Thus, I am expecting much fewer catastrophe losses for TRV when they report it next quarter - unless we get another hurricane.

Since this quarter was a blowout, I would like to see how the next quarter compares. I would like to see the company maintain decent top-line growth of around 8% while increasing its ROE and decreasing its combined ratio further to solidify itself as a great company that is capable of generating above-average profits.

Am I going to Invest in TRV?

In my view, the quarter was excellent mainly because the same quarter last year was terrible. I am not expecting the next quarter to be that positive for The Travelers Companies, Inc. Over 200% y/y growth in net income per share isn't difficult to achieve when the comps are easy. I would like to see how these numbers compare sequentially, and after the easy comps are out of the way. Can the company sustain its mid-80s underlying combined ratio going forward and average over 20% ROE? It is too early to tell still, and one great quarter doesn't tell me a lot. So, I would like to see the company maintaining this momentum going forward.

TRV has also been generous in rewarding its shareholders via share buybacks and dividends, which have been very steady. The dividend yield is still quite low, which, to me, just says that there is a lot of room for growth in the future. The company has grown its dividend for 18 years straight, so if the dividend is what you invest for primarily, and you have a long horizon ahead, it may be a decent, safe bet.

Now, in terms of buybacks, the company repurchased 1.1m shares this quarter and is on track to repurchase around 4m by the end of the year. That means the company purchases less than 2% a year, which I don't think is too bad. The company consistently repurchased shares for the last decade and reduced its share count by almost 33%. Consistent dividend growth and share repurchases tell me that the management is confident in the business and wants to reward shareholders.

However, I would like to do a lot more research on the insurance business and the company itself before committing any capital. I wanted to share my thoughts on the company and hear your thoughts on the subject and what you think about the company's efforts in rewarding its shareholders and overall operations.

data.title: Benitec Biopharma: OPMD Program Continues To Advance With Catalysts
data.description:
Benitec Biopharma Inc. (NASDAQ:BNTC) recently reported positive results from its phase 1b/2a clinical study using BB-301 for the treatment of patients with Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy [OPMD]. It reported these positive interim results at two time points during 2024 showing initial proof-of-concept that this gene therapy works in being able to treat these patients with this rare disorder.

Why should investors care about this program though now that these catalysts have come and gone? It is because this positive data allows the company to move the 3rd patient into the same dosing regimen in October 2024 for starters. Secondly, it allows it to move to higher dosing of BB-301 in 2025, which may ultimately yield an improvement in efficacy.

Speaking of which, it is highly likely that there should be the release of data from such higher dosing during this period as well. If all goes smoothly with this program regarding low and high-dosing, then it will be in good shape to initiate a late-stage study for this program in 2026. With the ability to advance BB-301 as a gene therapy treatment option for patients with OPMD, plus several catalysts on the way within the next few years, I believe that investors can capitalize on any potential gains made here.

BB-301 For The Treatment Of Patients With OPMD

As I noted above in the beginning, the main clinical program from this company would be the development of gene therapy BB-301 for the treatment of patients with Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy. The use of this therapy is being explored in the ongoing phase 1b/2a study. Before going over this specific trial, plus any catalysts to come out of this program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for it could be.

Ocularpharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy [OPMD] is a rare genetic disorder that leads to muscle weakness. Having said that, it is a type of muscular dystrophy. However, OPMD only affects the muscles of the throat and eyelids. This is a hard disorder to detect because someone who has it doesn't likely get diagnosed with it until they are 40 years of age. The thing to note is that it is a progressive type of disorder that just continues to get worse over time. However, while it does progress in such a fashion, it does so at a slow pace.

Some symptoms that these patients with OPMD experience are as follows:

  • Unable to speak
  • Double vision
  • Facial weakness
  • Impaired eye movement
  • Weakness of the tongue
  • Dysphagia [Difficulty in swallowing].

The global Oculopharyngeal Muscular Dystrophy market size is expected to surpass $10 billion by the end of 2036. This is a good market size and the hope is that this company can continue to advance this program. What exactly causes this disorder? It is caused by a mutation of the PABPN1 gene, which is inherited either by one or both parents. Even though one can have it in either of these scenarios, patients who obtain two copies of mutated PABN1 gene develop more severe symptoms earlier in the process.

To see if BB-301 can help treat OPMD patients, it is running the phase 1b/2a clinical study. This particular trial has recruited a total of 2 patients thus far and then a 3rd patient is expected to be enrolled during the month of October 2024. There was positive interim data analysis from 1 patient reported at the 90-day and 180-day time points. The improvements observed as this one patient received one dose of BB-301 is as follows:

  • 90-day post treatment period — improvement in swallowing function
  • 180-day post treatment period — TPR was meaningfully reduced [in essence, smaller amounts of food in the pharynx for patients taking the gene therapy compared to an OPMD natural history study].

In terms of safety, BB-301 was shown to be very safe. Matter of fact, the only adverse event [AE] noted in this particular trial from either of these two patients was a Grade 2 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease [GERD]. Even better than this, there were no serious adverse events [SAEs] reported from either of these two particular patients enrolled. The third patient remains an unknown for now. Thus, it is too early to say whether there will be any safety issues to be released later on.

Again, even though these set of results were already released from this ongoing phase 1b/2a clinical study, there are still a few other catalysts that investors can consider. Some of which may or may not ultimately yield a higher stock price. The first of which is that there will be the release of updated results, from this mid-stage trial using higher dosing of BB-301 for OPMD patients, in 2025. The reason this is possible is that the company is going to use higher dosing of BB-301 for the treatment of these patients during this period. From there, if everything goes smoothly, then it is expected that Benitec will be in a position to initiate a late-stage pivotal study for this particular program.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Benitec Biopharma had cash and cash equivalents of $50.9 million as of June 30th of 2024. The reason for the cash on hand is that it did a PIPE financing agreement back in April 2024. This was an oversubscribed private placement financing agreement of $40 million done on April 22nd of 2024. This was a crucial financing event, especially considering that it greatly extended its cash runway. It now believes that it has a cash runway, or enough cash on hand to fund its operations, through 2025. It has a cash burn $11.3 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into the spending of $7.8 million in R&D expenses and then $3.5 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Benitec Biopharma. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the higher dosing of BB-301 for the treatment of patients with OPMD. The goal is to test out higher doses of this gene therapy in 2025 to see if efficacy can be improved upon. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that moving to a higher dose of BB-301 will result in an improved clinical outcome.

The second risk is in terms of safety because there is no assurance that the third patient enrolled in the lower dosing of BB-301 will not experience any type of SAE. In addition, there is no way of knowing whether the higher dosing will have safety issues upon trial completion. If any safety issues are found in the higher dosing of BB-301, then that would force the company into a strategy of only moving to a pivotal study with a lower dose.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of advancing BB-301 for the treatment of patients with OPMD on the regulatory front. It is the intention of the company to move the development of this program forward towards pivotal phase 3 testing. Despite obtaining positive data from the ongoing phase 1b/2a study, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to allow it to move towards initiating such a trial. Nor, that the final results to be released from such a late-stage study will be positive.

Conclusion

Benitec Biopharma has done well to continue to advance its lead gene therapy BB-301 for the treatment of patients with OPMD. That's because it already reported positive interim analysis from one patient at day 90 and 180 from the phase 1b/2a study using this gene therapy to treat these patients. Not only that, but it posted positive interim data again afterward, showing that two patients had achieved improvement in terms of swallow function. This was the first gene therapy of its kind to help OPMD patients regarding this improvement.

The premise now is that the company still has plenty of catalysts on the way. In 2025, there will be the recruitment of higher dosing of BB-301 for OPMD. Plus, likely data to be released occasionally during the same year. As long as this program runs smoothly with higher dosing, then the company should be on track to get a pivotal phase 3 study going in 2026.
